scenario: A report-generation process must run every Friday at 6 PM Sofia time. The team prefers cron syntax over ISO 8601.
question: Which Timer Cycle cron expression fits?

options.text: 0 0 18 ? 3 CronExpression with six fields: seconds, minutes, hours, day-of-month, month, day-of-week. The ? character and named tokens like FRI are both accepted by Spring's parser for the day-of-week field. This expression fires at 18:00 every Friday, matching the scenario. The timezone can be configured separately in the cluster or process.

0 18 5 -- standard UNIX cron format with five fields: minutes, hours, day-of-month, month, day-of-week. 3 CronExpression format with six fields where the first field represents seconds. UNIX cron's five-field format omits the seconds field entirely, making it structurally incompatible with Camunda 8's cron parser, which expects a six-field expression.

Camunda 8 Timer Events do not support cron syntax and only accept ISO 8601 duration and date-time formats. This is factually incorrect. 3 CronExpression (six-field, seconds first), and ISO 8601 date-time for one-time timers. The cron option is fully supported alongside ISO 8601.

every Friday at 18:00 written as a natural-language phrase. Camunda 8 Timer Events do not include a natural-language expression parser. 3 six-field cron expressions. Natural-language scheduling phrases are not a valid input format for any Timer Event type in the Zeebe engine.

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8 supports Spring 5.3 CronExpression with 6 fields: seconds minutes hours day-of-month month day-of-week (per timer-events.md:104 linking the Spring 5.3 CronExpression Tutorial (https://spring.io/blog/2020/11/10/new-in-spring-5-3-improved-cron-expressions)). Spring 5.3 accepts both ? and as "no specific value" for day-of-month/day-of-week, so 0 0 18 ? FRI parses. Camunda's own documented example uses instead: 0 0 9-17 MON-FRI (per timer-events.md:106); the idiomatic Camunda form for the scenario is therefore 0 0 18 * FRI.

b.text: Incorrect. UNIX cron (5 fields, no seconds) isn't the Camunda 8 format.

c.text: Incorrect. Cron is supported alongside ISO 8601.

Spring 5.3 accepts both ? and as "no specific value" for day-of-month/day-of-week, so 0 0 18 ? FRI parses. Camunda's own documented example uses instead: 0 0 9-17 MON-FRI (per timer-events.md:106); the idiomatic Camunda form for the scenario is therefore 0 0 18 * FRI.
